title: Carving from windfall: a primer
subtitle: Finding, storing, and using fallen branches for green-wood projects
excerpt: A short reading on where to find good carving wood without buying it. Windfall species ranked for usefulness, how to assess a branch on the ground, how to store green billets so they keep working, and the legal position on collecting from public spaces.
sourceNotes: Beeton's Book of Household Management on rural windfall practice. Cassell's Cyclopaedia of Mechanics on green-wood storage. UK Forestry Commission public guidance on collection from public woodlands.

content.text: Most carving wood does not need to be bought.
Windfall
, coppice offcuts, and tree-surgeon prunings provide more than enough material for a year of carving projects. This reading covers what to look for, how to collect responsibly, and how to store
green wood
so it stays workable until you reach for it.
Best species, ranked
Not all wood carves equally well. The order below is for general-purpose carving (spoons, kuksas, treen, small mallets); the willow and basketry list is separate.

Sycamore. The treen all-rounder. Carves cleanly green, dries food-safe pale, takes a linseed finish well. Common windfall in UK parks and avenues; the seed (the maple key) is a giveaway in late summer.
Birch. The kuksa wood. Slightly softer than sycamore, very pale, dries quickly. Birch is also good for pyrography blanks.
Hazel. Coppice species. Excellent for tool handles and mallets; carves easily green and seasons to a hard pale wood. Also the basketry rod for hurdles and fences.
Cherry. Slightly denser than sycamore, warm pink tone. Takes a linseed finish that develops a deep glow with age. Windfall is rarer; community-orchard prunings are the reliable source.
Apple and pear. Fruitwood; tight grain, fine carving wood for small treen. Smaller pieces typical; rarely available as a long billet.
Lime/basswood. The relief-carver's standard. Fine grain, soft, holds detail. Common in old parkland. Often used for figurative carving over kitchen treen.
Ash. The handle wood. Slightly coarser grain than sycamore but stronger; the choice for axe handles, hammer hafts, and tool stalks. Common UK windfall.
Oak. Dense, hard, slow to carve but excellent for tent pegs, mallets, and small box work. Windfall oak is rarer; a coppice rotation or a fallen-branch find in old woodland is the source.
Avoid these species

Yew is toxic in dust and sap. Never carve yew for food contact or skin contact. Pine carves well but is resinous and flavours food. Not for treen. Walnut stains black against iron tools and is a tree-nut allergen. Use with care. Reclaimed timber of unknown history is never safe for handled pieces. That includes paint, treatments, MDF, and plywood.
Assessing a branch on the ground
A fallen branch is useful for carving if it is fresh, straight, and free of major flaws. Look for:
End-grain that is still moist. A freshly fallen branch shows a damp pale end-grain; a long-fallen one shows a dry darkened end with cracks radiating from the centre. Fresh is what you want.
Straight grain along the length. Sight along the branch from one end. A spiral or twist shows as the surface lines following a long curve; pass on twisted wood for carving (it makes good firewood).
Sound bark. Loose or peeling bark is a sign of decay underneath. The wood inside may still be sound; cut a small test slice to confirm the surface is firm and the colour is consistent.
No major knots in the working section. Small knots are workable; a knot the size of a thumbnail in the middle of a planned spoon blank is not.
The collection question

In the UK, the rule for public woodland is the four Fs. That means foliage, flowers, fruit, and fungi only, in small amounts. Branches are wood, not foliage. They belong to the landowner. In practice, Forestry Commission and Wildlife Trust woods accept small fallen branches for home use. Community orchards and council parks vary. The courtesy is to ask the warden first. Take only what you will use. Leave the larger fallen timber for habitat.

Tree-surgeon offcuts are an underused source. Surgeons cut large quantities of high-quality wood that goes for firewood or to the chipper; many are happy for a carver to take what they need, especially sycamore, ash, birch, and cherry that the maker can identify from the bark and end-grain. Introduce yourself, offer to help load the truck, leave a card.
Storing green wood
Green wood loses moisture from the ends faster than from the sides, which causes
checking
. To slow checking and keep the
billet
workable:
Seal the end-grain with paraffin wax, PVA glue, or even old paint. End-sealing buys 2 to 4 weeks of working time.
Store in a cool shaded place: a shed floor, a corner of the garage, under a north-facing eaves. Avoid sun and heat sources.
For longer storage, bury the billets in damp wood shavings inside a closed box or bag. The shavings hold moisture and slow drying further; this can extend working time to 3 months or more.
For the longest storage, freeze the billet in a polythene bag. Carving wood thaws in 6 hours and is ready to work; freezing arrests drying entirely.
When to use it

Each green-wood project lists its preferred wood state in the brief. Most spoon-carving, kuksa-making, basketry, and mallet work wants wood that is fully green or only slightly dried. Once a billet has dried below 15 percent moisture, it carves like seasoned wood. At that point it needs the chisel-and-mallet methods of joinery, not the axe-and-knife methods of spoon-carving.

A small moisture meter from a hardware shop reads through bark and tells you what you have. The reading drifts by species but the rough rule holds: above 25 percent is wet green; 15 to 25 percent is workable green; below 15 percent is seasoned and carves harder under a knife.
